lesson_title: Overloading the increment and decrement operators

cards.text: Prefix increment ({{c1::++x}}) increments the value and returns the {{c2::new value}}, while postfix increment ({{c3::x++}}) increments but returns the {{c4::old value}}.
To distinguish prefix from postfix, postfix operators take a dummy {{c1::int}} parameter: operator++({{c2::int}}).

cards.question: What does this prefix ++ operator do?
cards.answer: Increments m_cents and returns a reference to *this (the updated object). This is prefix ++.
cards.explanation: Prefix ++ modifies the object and returns a reference for chaining and efficiency.

cards.question: What does this postfix ++ operator do?
cards.answer: Saves old value, increments *this, returns the old value. This is postfix ++.
cards.explanation: Postfix returns old value by value. The int parameter distinguishes it from prefix.

cards.front: Why does prefix ++ return by reference while postfix ++ returns by value?
cards.back: Prefix returns the modified object itself (by reference) for efficiency. Postfix must return the old value before increment, which requires creating a copy (return by value).

cards.text:
Prefix operator++ signature: ClassName{{c1::&}} operator{{c2::++}}().
Postfix operator++ signature: ClassName operator++({{c3::int}}).

cards.front: Which is more efficient: prefix or postfix increment? Why?
cards.back: Prefix (++x) is more efficient because it doesn't create a copy. Postfix (x++) must save the old value before incrementing, requiring a copy. For primitive types compilers optimize this away, but not for user-defined types.

cards.text: The dummy int parameter in operator++(int) is {{c1::never used}} and has {{c2::no name}}. It only serves to {{c3::distinguish}} postfix from prefix.

cards.front: Can you implement postfix ++ using prefix ++?
cards.back:
Yes. Common pattern: save copy, call prefix ++(*this), return copy. This reduces code duplication:
Cents operator++(int) { Cents temp{*this}; ++(*this); return temp; }

cards.explanation:
Standard patterns:
  • Prefix (++x): Modify object, return *this by reference
  • Postfix (x++): Save copy, call prefix version, return old copy by value
  • Same applies to -- operators
  • Prefer prefix for efficiency (no copy)
  • Must be member functions
examples: T operator++(int) { T temp{*this}; ++(*this); return temp; }  // postfix

cards.text: Increment and decrement operators should be implemented as {{c1::member functions}}, not friend functions.

cards.front: What's the difference between operator-- and operator++ implementation?
cards.back: They're identical except one decrements and one increments. Prefix --: decrement and return *this by reference. Postfix --: save copy, decrement, return old copy.
